era_context: The early Victorian era (1837–1901) is well established in Britain. The Industrial Revolution accelerates in Western Europe and North America. The Qing Dynasty in China faces mounting internal and external pressures. The Ottoman Empire continues its decline. In the Americas, westward expansion and slavery dominate political discourse. This is an era of rapid technological innovation, imperial competition, and the emergence of nationalist movements.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited European documentation; oral traditions underrepresented)

events.title: Treaty of Waitangi Signed; British Sovereignty Established in New Zealand
events.description: On February 6, 1840, the Treaty of Waitangi is signed between the British Crown and Māori chiefs at Waitangi in the Bay of Islands. The treaty is intended to establish British sovereignty over New Zealand while guaranteeing Māori rights to land and resources. However, the treaty exists in two versions—English and Māori—with significant discrepancies in translation and interpretation. The English version grants sovereignty to the Crown; the Māori version (te Tiriti o Waitangi) is more ambiguous. These differences will fuel disputes for centuries. The treaty marks the formal beginning of British colonization of New Zealand and the displacement of Māori populations.
key_figures: Hongi Hika (Māori chief, deceased 1828 but legacy influences negotiations)

sources.name: Treaty of Waitangi (1840), primary diplomatic text in English and Māori
British Colonial Office records and correspondence
Māori oral traditions and later recorded accounts

events.certainty_note: The treaty is a primary diplomatic document with contemporary signatures and official records. The date (February 6, 1840) is definitively established.

events.title: First Opium War Begins; British Naval Forces Attack China
events.description: The First Opium War (1840–1842) begins when British naval forces attack Chinese ports and coastal installations. The war is ostensibly fought over Britain's right to trade opium in China, but it reflects deeper tensions over Western commercial access to Chinese markets and China's resistance to foreign trade. The British East India Company and private merchants have been smuggling opium into China for decades, creating a massive addiction crisis and draining silver from the Chinese economy. When the Chinese imperial commissioner Lin Zexu attempts to suppress the opium trade by confiscating British opium stocks in Canton (Guangzhou), Britain responds with military force. The war demonstrates Western military and naval superiority and will result in China's humiliating defeat and the imposition of unequal treaties.
key_figures: Lin Zexu (Chinese imperial commissioner, anti-opium advocate)

sources.name: British East India Company records and naval dispatches
Qing Dynasty official records (Qing Shilu) and Lin Zexu's memorials
British Parliamentary debates and correspondence

events.certainty_note: The outbreak of the First Opium War in 1840 is well-documented in British naval records, East India Company archives, and Chinese official histories.

events.title: William Henry Harrison Elected U.S. President; 'Tippecanoe and Tyler Too'
events.description: William Henry Harrison, a Whig candidate and former military officer, is elected the 9th President of the United States in November 1840. His campaign slogan, 'Tippecanoe and Tyler Too,' references his military victory at the Battle of Tippecanoe (1811) and his running mate, John Tyler. Harrison's election represents a Whig victory over the incumbent Democratic Party and President Martin Van Buren, who is blamed for the economic depression of the late 1830s. Harrison's presidency will be cut short by his death in April 1841, just 31 days into his term, making him the first president to die in office.
key_figures: William Henry Harrison (Whig candidate, elected president)
Martin Van Buren (incumbent Democratic president, defeated)

sources.name: U.S. Electoral records and contemporary newspapers
Historiographic consensus on 1840 election

events.certainty_note: The 1840 U.S. presidential election is well-documented in electoral records, newspapers, and historical archives.

events.title: Queen Victoria Marries Prince Albert
events.description: On February 10, 1840, Queen Victoria marries her cousin Prince Albert of Saxe-Coburg and Gotha. The marriage is a love match, unusual for royal unions of the era, and Victoria's devotion to Albert becomes legendary. The marriage produces nine children, whose marriages link Victoria to royal houses across Europe. Albert becomes a major influence on British culture and technology, championing the Great Exhibition of 1851. The marriage is celebrated in Britain and becomes a symbol of Victorian domesticity and morality. Victoria's grief after Albert's death in 1861 will profoundly shape the remainder of her reign.

sources.name: Royal records and contemporary accounts of the wedding
Queen Victoria's journals and letters
Contemporary British newspapers and periodicals

events.certainty_note: The date and details of Victoria and Albert's marriage are extensively documented in royal records and contemporary sources.

events.title: Penny Black Stamp Issued; First Adhesive Postage Stamp
events.description: On May 1, 1840, the Penny Black becomes the world's first adhesive postage stamp, issued by the British Post Office. Designed by William Mulready and featuring a portrait of Queen Victoria, the Penny Black revolutionizes postal communication by allowing prepayment of postage through an adhesive stamp. The innovation is part of Rowland Hill's postal reform, which standardizes postage rates and makes mail delivery more efficient and accessible. The Penny Black becomes an iconic symbol of the Victorian era and marks a major advance in communication technology and infrastructure.

sources.name: British Post Office records and official announcements
Philatelic archives and stamp collections
Contemporary British newspapers and periodicals

events.certainty_note: The Penny Black's issue date (May 1, 1840) is definitively established in Post Office records and philatelic documentation.

events.title: Chartist Movement Peaks; Mass Petition to Parliament
events.description: The Chartist movement, a working-class political movement demanding democratic reforms, reaches a peak in 1840 with mass rallies and the presentation of a massive petition to Parliament. The Chartists demand universal male suffrage, secret ballots, annual parliaments, and other democratic reforms. Although the movement is ultimately unsuccessful in achieving its immediate goals, it represents the first mass working-class political movement in Britain and influences the development of trade unionism and democratic socialism. The movement's decline after 1840 reflects both government repression and the gradual expansion of the franchise through other means.

sources.name: Chartist newspapers and pamphlets (Northern Star, etc.)
British Parliamentary records and Home Office correspondence
Contemporary British newspapers and periodicals

events.certainty_note: The Chartist movement's activities in 1840 are well-documented in contemporary newspapers, parliamentary records, and Chartist publications.

events.title: Māori Wars Period Begins; Hone Heke Challenges British Authority
events.description: In the months following the Treaty of Waitangi (February 1840), tensions escalate between British settlers and Māori communities over land and resource rights. Hone Heke, a prominent Māori chief in the Bay of Islands, begins to challenge British authority and the treaty's implementation. While open conflict does not erupt until 1845, the period from 1840 onward is marked by increasing friction, disputes over land surveys, and Māori resistance to European settlement. The seeds of the Māori Wars (1845–1872) are sown in this period.
key_figures: Hongi Hika (deceased 1828, but legacy influences Hone Heke)

sources.name: British Colonial Office records and correspondence
Māori oral traditions and later recorded accounts
Historiographic consensus on Māori Wars (e.g., James Belich, The New Zealand Wars)

events.certainty_note: The escalation of tensions in 1840 is documented in British colonial records, though exact dating of Māori resistance movements can be uncertain due to reliance on oral traditions.

events.title: Slavery Abolished in French Colonies
events.description: France abolishes slavery in its colonies in 1840, following the British abolition in 1833. The French abolition is less comprehensive than the British version and includes provisions for compensation to slave owners. The abolition reflects growing anti-slavery sentiment in Europe and the influence of abolitionist movements. However, the transition from slavery to indentured labor systems in French colonies creates new forms of exploitation. The abolition is part of a broader trend of European powers ending slavery, though the slave trade continues illegally in some regions.
key_figures: Victor Schoelcher (French abolitionist, though major work comes later)

sources.name: French government decrees and official records
French parliamentary records and debates

events.certainty_note: French abolition of slavery in 1840 is documented in official government records and parliamentary proceedings.

disconfirming_evidence: The Treaty of Waitangi is often presented as a clear agreement establishing British sovereignty, but modern scholarship emphasizes the profound translation discrepancies between the English and Māori versions. The Māori version does not clearly cede sovereignty in the same way as the English version, suggesting that Māori signatories may not have understood themselves as surrendering independence. This ambiguity is not a minor technical issue but a fundamental dispute that shapes New Zealand history. Additionally, the characterization of the First Opium War as purely a British imperialist aggression is complicated by evidence that some Chinese merchants and officials benefited from the opium trade and that internal Chinese political factions had divergent interests. The war was not simply imposed on a unified China but occurred within a context of internal Chinese political conflict.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1840 is rich in Western sources, particularly British colonial records, naval dispatches, and parliamentary proceedings. Chinese sources exist but are often filtered through later nationalist historiography. The historical record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic and social history of non-elite populations is underrepresented. Māori perspectives are largely absent from surviving written records, with oral traditions recorded much later and often filtered through European intermediaries. The record reflects Victorian-era biases toward imperial expansion and technological progress. Significant gaps exist in documentation of internal political events in non-Western societies, particularly in Sub-Saharan Africa, Central Asia, and the Pacific Islands.

graph_edges.note: Treaty of Waitangi leads to accelerated land dispossession
First Opium War continues; Treaty of Nanking signed in 1841–1842
Harrison's election leads to his presidency and death in 1841
Victoria and Albert's marriage leads to Albert's influence on Great Exhibition
